overview.title: Mi caminata en Nueva Zelanda
overview.topic: A solo hike through valleys and hills, ending exhausted at a refuge

notes.heading: Ver in the preterite vs. imperfect
notes.body: 'Vi el lago' means I caught sight of it — a single moment of perception, completed and discrete. 'Veía el lago' means I was looking at it / it was in my view — an ongoing sensory experience. English 'I saw' covers both, which is why this distinction trips up English speakers. The rule: use preterite for the instant something registers; use imperfect for the continuous experience of perceiving it.

notes.heading: Simultaneous ongoing actions with mientras
notes.body: Sentence 4 stacks three imperfects inside a mientras frame: caminaba, escuchaba, sentía. All three are simultaneous ongoing sensory states with no clear beginning or end. When multiple background actions overlap in time, all of them take the imperfect. Only an interruption or a completed event within them would switch to the preterite.

notes.heading: Quedarse dormido as a change-of-state preterite
notes.body: 'Me quedé dormido' in the preterite expresses the moment of falling asleep — a punctual change of state, not an ongoing condition. Compare with 'estaba dormido' (imperfect), which describes the state of being asleep. Quedarse, ponerse, volverse, and hacerse are all change-of-state verbs that typically appear in the preterite when describing the instant the change occurred.

notes.heading: Sequential preterites to close a narrative
notes.body: The final sentence chains three preterites (llegué, quité, me quedé) describing end-of-day actions one after another. Sequential completed actions narrated in order all take the preterite. The chain reads like a checklist of events that happened and are done. This contrasts sharply with sentence 4's three simultaneous imperfects, and putting them back-to-back in the story makes the structural difference visceral.

sentences.translation: The trail was steep and the wind was blowing hard when I climbed the first hill.
rationale: era → imperfecto: describes a permanent characteristic of the trail — background scene-setting, not a change.
soplaba → imperfecto: the wind blowing was an ongoing condition already in progress, part of the atmosphere I walked into.
subí → pretérito: climbing the first hill was a single, bounded, completed action with a clear start and end.

sentences.translation: From the summit, I saw a blue lake that shone among the mountains like a mirror.
parts: entre las montañas como un espejo.
rationale: vi → pretérito: seeing the lake was a single, completed perceptual event — the moment I looked and it registered.
brillaba → imperfecto: the lake shining was a continuous ongoing state that existed independently — I arrived into it, I didn't cause it to begin.

sentences.translation: Suddenly, some sheep crossed the path and I had to wait several minutes.
rationale: cruzaron → pretérito: de repente signals a sudden, punctual interruption — the sheep crossing was a discrete completed event.
tuve → pretérito: tener que + infinitive in the preterite means 'had to' as a specific obligation that arose and was fulfilled at that moment, not a habitual state.

sentences.translation: While I was walking through the valley, I listened to the sound of the river and smelled the scent of pine.
rationale: caminaba → imperfecto: mientras introduces an ongoing background action — walking through the valley was the continuous frame.
escuchaba → imperfecto: hearing the river was a simultaneous, ongoing sensory experience, not a single moment.
sentía → imperfecto: smelling the pine was also a continuous sensory state layered into the same background moment.

sentences.translation: At the end of the day, I arrived at the shelter exhausted, took off my boots, and fell asleep in seconds.
rationale: llegué → pretérito: arriving at the refuge was a single, completed event that closed out the day.
quité → pretérito: taking off the boots was a discrete, sequential completed action — one thing after another.
me quedé → pretérito: quedarse dormido in the preterite expresses the moment of falling asleep — a punctual change of state, not an ongoing condition.
